lesson.menu_desc: Handling tension with care: open a hard talk without triggering defensiveness, soften the message with tentative language, stay candid but constructive, and protect the relationship — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 — Difficult Conversations — Handling Tension with Care
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 10 — Difficult Conversations

listenings.text: Okay, in ten minutes I have to talk to Bruno about last quarter, and I am not looking forward to it. His numbers dropped, two accounts slipped, and I need to address it honestly. But I have learned something the hard way: if I open with You missed your targets, he will get defensive, shut down, and nothing will change. So let me plan this. I will not lead with the problem; I will lead with a question. I was hoping we could look at the quarter together, and then, what do you think got in the way? Listen first. Show some empathy before I say anything hard. He is a good person having a rough run, not a lazy one. Then I will be candid about the gap, because pretending it is fine helps no one, but I will keep it constructive: we talk about fixes, not blame. My job is to defuse the tension, not to win. If I get this right, he owns up to what happened, we agree on a clear next step, and his morale survives. Be assertive about the issue, gentle with the person. That is the whole skill. Right. Let me go.

Hi Victor, it is Renata from People and Culture. Before your conversation with Bruno, three quick reminders. First, separate the person from the problem. Be candid about the result, but never attack the person; the moment someone feels judged, they stop listening and start defending. So describe what you saw, not who he is. Second, lead with a question, not a verdict. Ask what has been getting in his way and actually listen to the answer. A little empathy early does more to defuse tension than any clever phrase. And use tentative language to soften the opening: I was hoping we could, I was wondering if, it would help if. It lowers the temperature without hiding the message. Third, do not end the talk in the air. Agree on one clear next step together, so he can own up to what happened and walk out with his morale intact. Address the issue, clear the air, and protect the relationship. You are good at this, Victor. Go in calm, stay curious, and you will be fine.
